Title: Ex-NST journalist Abdul Aziz Hassan dies at 71
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Former New Straits Times journalist Abdul Aziz Hassan died this morning.
He was 71.
A graduate in Mass Communications from Universiti Teknologi Mara (then ITM), Abdul Aziz was known for his contributions to journalism.
His remains will be brought to Masjid Al-Azim in Pandan Indah here before being laid to rest at the Pandan Indah Muslim cemetery.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Cathay Pacific sees growing Malaysia traffic as Hong Kong strengthens its hub role
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Cathay Pacific is seeing continued growth in demand from Malaysia from both passenger travel and cargo services supported by growing connectivity through Hong Kong and continued exports of electronics and semiconductors.
The airline's area head, Singapore and Malaysia, Nicolas Masse, said Cathay Pacific has added a fourth daily flight between Kuala Lumpur and Hong Kong from March 30, 2026, signalling the strong passenger travel demand.
"What has been encouraging is that demand built up very quickly. Normally, a new flight takes several months to establish itself and build awareness among travellers.
"However, during its first months of operation, load factors were already very strong. That's always pleasing because it indicates that demand exists and customers value the additional schedule flexibility," he told Business Times.
Masse added that Malaysia also continues to benefit from demand linked to artificial intelligence (AI) growth, data centres and semiconductor manufacturing, given that the country has established itself as an electronics manufacturing hub.
"The AI boom, electronics exports and data centres all require semiconductors and AI chips," he said, adding that cargo customers continue telling him that shipments need to be made to various destinations, including Singapore, China and New York.
Cathay Pacific also operates dedicated freighter services from Penang to cater to the growing demand of cargo services from Malaysia.
The cargo, similar to passengers, would transit in Hong Kong before being moved to the final destination.
Masse said Cathay Pacific is continuing to operate normally despite disruptions in the Middle East airspace and has seen travellers use Hong Kong as an alternative transit point.
However, he believed that Hong Kong's readiness in welcoming more passengers with the third runway opened at Hong Kong International Airport (HKIA) in Nov 2024 is key to strengthening the country's position as a transit hub.
"Over the last few years, HKIA has added a third runway…That gives passengers more transit options because Hong Kong is now an even larger aviation hub."
"That development was already planned. I wouldn't say the Middle East situation has fundamentally changed Hong Kong's long-term role. Hong Kong remains strategically positioned in Asia, connecting Malaysia to the rest of the world," he said.
Beyond network growth, Cathay Pacific continues to invest in its premium offerings as competition for long-haul travel intensifies.
"Long-haul travel is about the overall experience. Customers are looking for more than just competitive fares. They're looking for a premium experience," Masse said.
"We're now extending cabin upgrades beyond our long-haul fleet. The Airbus A330 regional fleet is also being upgraded with new cabin products, including flat-bed seats."
The A350 has already been very successful, and now we're focusing on enhancing the regional experience as well," he said.
He added that reliability is important for Cathay Pacific, as the airline acknowledged that passengers would not want their flights to be abruptly changed numerous times.
"We've already announced the extension of suspensions to Dubai, but for the rest of the network we've made it clear that we're not going to make last-minute changes. July and August are critical travel months because of the northern summer season.
"Families have holidays planned, students are travelling, and people expect certainty. That's why we publicly committed to safeguarding our network during that period. If a flight is published in our schedule, our intention is to operate it," he said.
Despite ongoing geopolitical tensions and fuel price volatility, Masse said Cathay Pacific remains well positioned to navigate uncertainties.
Although fuel remains its largest expense, accounting for about 30 per cent of its cost, the airline continues to be agile and disciplined in how it operates.
Having a strong balance sheet is key for an airline to survive any turbulence coming its way.
"Cathay Pacific is in a strong financial position. We've remained profitable and have maintained financial discipline. If you look back at Covid-19, one of the key lessons was that airlines need strong balance sheets and sufficient liquidity to withstand shocks," Masse said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bursa Malaysia ends on softer note amid profit-taking
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Bursa Malaysia ended on a softer note today as investors engaged in profit-taking following the recent rebound in the local market, an analyst said.
At 5pm, the FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI (FBM KLCI) declined by 0.65 per cent, or 11.19 points, to 1,700.84 from last Friday's close of 1,712.03.
The benchmark index opened 1.56 points lower at 1,710.47 and moved between 1,699.94 and 1,712.32 throughout the trading session.
Market breadth was negative, with decliners outnumbering gainers 560 to 481.
A total of 608 counters were unchanged, 1,649 untraded, and 14 suspended.
Turnover slipped to 3.29 billion units worth RM2.40 billion from 3.45 billion units worth RM3.79 billion on Friday.
Regionally, key indices closed broadly higher, supported by strong buying interest in technology stocks as investors continued to position for long-term growth opportunities arising from artificial intelligence (AI), said Rakuten Trade Sdn Bhd vice-president of equity research Thong Pak Leng.
He said strong capital inflows into the technology sector reflected investors' confidence that demand for AI-related hardware and semiconductor solutions remain resilient.
On the domestic front, Thong remained cautious as geopolitical tensions between the United States and Iran remain unresolved, with peace negotiations remaining fragile.
"Nevertheless, the local market continues to be supported by bargain-hunting in selected blue-chip counters and improving sentiment across regional markets.
"Overall, we expect the FBM KLCI to remain range-bound in the near term, with the benchmark index likely to trade between 1,690 and 1,720 this week," he told Bernama.
Among the heavyweights, Maybank shed 22 sen to RM10.96, Public Bank eased 5.0 sen to RM4.90, Tenaga was flat at RM14.50, CIMB declined 2.0 sen to RM7.63, and IHH Healthcare was 9.0 sen easier at RM8.71.
Of the most active stocks, Tanco edged down one sen to 13 sen, Zetrix AI rose half a sen to 78.5 sen, Dagang NeXchange was flat at 46.5 sen, GIIB slid 4.5 sen to 42 sen, and SKP Resources lost half a sen to 27.5 sen.
Among the top gainers, UMS Integration rose 32 sen to RM9.02, SAM Engineering jumped 29 sen to RM5.19, Skyechip surged 27 sen to RM3.36, TMK Chemical soared 21 sen to RM2.75, and Kee Ming garnered 12 sen to RM1.47.
As for the top losers, Malaysian Pacific Industries slipped RM1.06 to RM48.84, Nestle declined 92 sen to RM95.30, United Plantations sank 70 sen to RM31.96, PPB slid 50 sen to RM9.62, and Allianz fell 40 sen to RM21.20.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Traffic compounds for over 700 offences set to increase to RM500
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The maximum compound rate for more than 700 road traffic offences is expected to increase to RM500 if amendments to the Road Transport Act 1987 (Act 333) are passed by parliament.
The Transport Ministry is expected to table the proposed increase during the first reading of the Road Transport (Amendment) Bill at the Second Meeting of the Fifth Session of the 15th Parliament, which begins today and runs until July 16.
Currently, the maximum compound for various traffic offences issued by the Road Transport Department (RTD) and police, including reckless driving, speeding, driving without a valid licence or road tax, and running red lights, stands at RM300.
However, the existing rates are viewed as inadequate as serious and repeat offences continue to occur and are often taken lightly by some road users.
It is understood that the proposed increase would involve offences under Section 14 relating to the licensing of motor vehicles, Section 15 on motor vehicle licences, Section 26 concerning the use of vehicles without a valid driving licence, and Section 40 on compulsory third-party insurance.
The amendments would also cover Section 59 on the inspection of vehicles, Section 66A involving registration number offences, and Section 72 concerning illegal parking and other parking-related offences.
In addition, the proposed increase would affect offences under Section 76A relating to specific traffic enforcement matters, Section 79 involving failure to comply with traffic signs or directions, Section 102 concerning obstruction of enforcement officers, and Section 120 on general offences and penalties.
A source said the proposed amendments were aimed at addressing serious and repeat traffic offences that pose risks to other road users, Berita Harian reported.
"The stricter measures proposed under the amendments to the Road Transport Act 1987 are intended to improve compliance and enhance road safety among the public.
"However, the amendments do not propose setting all compounds at RM500. Instead, RTD will restructure the compound rates for more than 700 compoundable offences."
It is also understood that the amendments would introduce specific provisions to address road-related offences involving illegal street racers and 'mat rempit', which are currently difficult for enforcement authorities to tackle effectively.
"The Transport Ministry will introduce a new Section 42A to address illegal racing and speed-testing activities on public roads. The new provision will close existing legal loopholes and provide clearer enforcement powers.
"If illegal racers are found to be endangering the public, they can be immediately arrested and charged in court," the source said.
The amendments will also strengthen enforcement against 'tonto' activities through changes to Section 110B.
"The new provisions and amendments will define activities associated with 'tonto' groups, including gathering to watch races, obstructing roads and excessive honking or revving of exhausts.
"It will empower enforcement officers to order such gatherings to disperse, seize vehicles and detain those involved.
"Higher penalties, including heavier fines, imprisonment and licence suspension, will also be introduced," the source said.
The amendments will also focus on micromobility vehicles, which are increasingly being used on public roads, to ensure their safe operation.
"There will be requirements governing their use on roads and designated routes, including speed limits and minimum age requirements for users.
"The amendments will also empower enforcement authorities to take action and impose penalties for breaches of the regulations," the source said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Coffee break: Starbucks Korea stores pause for training after 'Tank Day' fiasco
Article:
SEOUL: Lights off. Doors closed. Coffee lovers across South Korea were left craving their fix on Monday as more than 2,000 Starbucks shops shut for staff training after a marketing fiasco.
Starbucks Korea – the coffee giant's third-largest market after the United States and China – was embroiled in a major controversy last month over a promotion that evoked painful memories of a 1980 government crackdown on pro-democracy activists.
The day of the reusable cup promotion – May 18 – coincided with the 46th anniversary of the Gwangju uprising in which 165 civilians were killed when tanks and troops were deployed, according to the official toll.
Many believe the number is much higher.
Starbucks Korea christened the occasion "Tank Day" to market its large tumblers, but its campaign was slammed as callous.
The controversy led to the dismissal of Starbucks Korea's CEO and prompted a public apology from the chairman of Shinsegae Group, which operates the chain under licence from Starbucks US headquarters.
To contain the backlash, the South Korean chain shut stores nationwide from 3:00 pm (0600 GMT) on Monday for staff to attend three hours of "historical and social awareness" training.
The sessions "focused on historical and social awareness" and a "commitment to the Starbucks mission and values", the company said.
Stores were to remain closed for the rest of the day, for which staff received their full pay.
Many coffee lovers – unaware of the early closure – were turned away at one outlet in central Seoul, where the blinds were drawn as staff watched an educational video inside.
Yoon Soo-nyung, a 26-year-old banker, was not impressed with the "over-the-top" measure.
"I suspect it is more of a performative, box-ticking gesture," he told AFP before heading off to find another coffee shop nearby.
'LAX' SYSTEM
Cho Seong-ha, a 45-year-old office worker, agreed the move appeared "showy".
"The problem stemmed from a mistake by the marketing team. I am clueless as to why employees making coffee in the field have to undergo this training," she said.
Some supported the measure.
Jang Yu-ra, an 18-year-old passerby, said the chain's decision showed it "clearly recognised the seriousness and responded accordingly".
"I think it is a good direction," she said.
Shinsegae had identified a series of negligent acts leading up to the promotion, including officials signing off without checking the design file.
The controversy sparked protests in Seoul and Gwangju and led to a "sharp decline in sales" in the early days of the scandal, according to the operator.
Yoon, the banker, said he could not understand how a company as big as Starbucks Korea could have allowed this to happen.
"Something like this should have been handled through an organisational system – all decisions go through a process, after all – but it seemed so poorly managed," he said.
"It just made me wonder how such a large corporation could be that lax."
Shinsegae chairman Chung Yong-jin and other senior executives will sit for the same history training on Wednesday.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: A decade of chaos: Britain prepares for seventh prime minister
Article:
BRITISH Prime Minister Keir Starmer said he would quit on Monday, paving the way for the country to have its seventh leader in 10 years.
The chaos dates back to the Brexit referendum, 10 years ago to the day on Tuesday.
In the years since the vote, Britain has tried to forge its own path but struggled to boost its low-growth economy, hamstrung by high debts and a growing welfare bill, at a time of growing geopolitical volatility.
JUNE 2016: UK VOTES FOR BREXIT, PM CAMERON QUITS
Britons cause a global shock by voting 52-48 per cent to leave the European Union, ending a more than 40-year union and plunging the country into its biggest political crisis since World War Two.
Conservative Prime Minister David Cameron resigns and the party chooses Theresa May to succeed him.
JUNE 2017: SNAP ELECTION GAMBLE BACKFIRES
Riding high in opinion polls and seeking a bigger majority in parliament to push Brexit legislation through, May calls a snap election.
The Conservatives lose their majority and form a government by striking a deal with Northern Ireland's pro-UK Democratic Unionist Party.
MAY 2019: BREXIT PARALYSIS, MAY RESIGNS, JOHNSON TAKES OVER
May quits after failing to break a parliamentary deadlock over how Britain should leave the EU. Boris Johnson, one of the main faces of the pro-Brexit campaign, wins the internal Conservative Party contest to succeed her.
DECEMBER 2019: JOHNSON LEADS CONSERVATIVES TO SWEEPING WIN
With parliament paralysed over Brexit, Johnson calls a snap election. Campaigning under the slogan "Get Brexit Done" he steers the Conservatives to their biggest election win since Margaret Thatcher's landslide victory in 1987.
JANUARY 2020: BREXIT GETS DONE
Johnson uses his mandate to drive a Brexit deal through parliament and Brussels, and Britain exits the EU on January 31, 2020, becoming the first state to withdraw from the bloc.
JULY 2022: JOHNSON OUSTED
Johnson leads Britain during the Covid-19 pandemic - at one point being hospitalised himself with the disease - but a long list of scandals and missteps proves too much and he steps down after a ministerial revolt.
SEPTEMBER 2022: TRUSS' CHAOTIC PREMIERSHIP
Liz Truss beats Rishi Sunak in a contest to succeed Johnson. Her "mini-budget" containing unfunded tax cuts spooks financial markets, pushing up borrowing costs sharply and further tarnishing Britain's reputation for political and fiscal stability. She lasts only 44 days before announcing her resignation.
OCTOBER 2022: SUNAK BECOMES PRIME MINISTER
Sunak takes over as Britain's third prime minister in as many months, pledging to restore stability to government. He makes five key pledges focused on the economy, stopping illegal immigration and improving the health system. In February 2023, Sunak strikes a deal with the EU on trade rules for Northern Ireland, improving ties with the bloc.
MAY 2024: SUNAK CALLS ELECTION
Trailing the Labour Party by around 20 points in the polls, Sunak calls an election for July 4.
JULY 2024: STARMER BECOMES PRIME MINISTER
"We said we would end the chaos and we will," Keir Starmer, leader of the Labour Party, told supporters on July 5, 2024, after winning a landslide election but with the smallest share of the electoral vote of any majority government in modern history.
[[nid:1469870]]
AUGUST 2024: STARMER WARNS 'THINGS WILL GET WORSE'
Starmer warns over the state of the public finances, saying the Labour Party has inherited "an economic black hole" and tells voters "things will get worse before they get better."
OCTOBER 2024: LABOUR'S FIRST BUDGET
Finance minister Rachel Reeves announces tax rises worth £40 billion (US$52.76 billion) a year, primarily by raising employers' social security contributions, bringing the tax burden to its highest level on record in peacetime and prompting an outcry from businesses.
FEBRUARY 2025: NIGEL FARAGE'S REFORM UK PARTY SURGES
Right-wing anti-immigration party Reform UK overtakes Labour in a national opinion poll for the first time. Reform UK, led by Brexit campaigner Nigel Farage, has topped polls ever since.
JUNE 2025: REBELLION FORCES STARMER U-TURN ON WELFARE
Starmer is forced to reverse plans to cut Britain's welfare bill after his own lawmakers threatened to defeat the government.
SEPTEMBER-APRIL 2025: MANDELSON SCANDAL
Pressure on Starmer ramps up over his appointment of Peter Mandelson as Britain's ambassador to Washington.
Mandelson was later sacked over his ties to the late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein, as questions emerge over Starmer's judgment and the vetting process involved.
MAY 2026: LOCAL ELECTION DISASTER
The Labour Party suffers heavy losses in English local elections and votes for the Scottish and Welsh assemblies, deepening questions over Starmer's ability to govern, with Reform UK the main beneficiary.
MAY 2026: WES STREETING RESIGNS AS HEALTH MINISTER
Health Minister Wes Streeting quits saying he had lost confidence in Starmer's leadership and calls for a leadership contest, in which he said he would hope to compete.
JUNE 2026: DEFENCE MINISTER JOHN HEALEY QUITS
British Defence Minister John Healey quits over a months-long dispute over defence spending, accusing Starmer of failing to commit the money needed to keep the country safe from mounting threats.
JUNE 2026: ANDY BURNHAM SHOWS HE CAN BEAT REFORM
UK Greater Manchester Mayor Andy Burnham wins an election in the north of England, trouncing Reform UK in the process, and allowing him to return to Westminster, removing a key obstacle to any leadership challenge against Starmer.
* The article is from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police continue probe into defamation against Tok Batin in Jaslinda disappearance case
Article:
IPOH: The investigation paper on a TikTok user accused of defaming and humiliating the Tok Batin of Kampung Lubuk Gaharu has been referred back to police by the Deputy Public Prosecutor's office for further investigation.
Perak police chief Datuk Mohd Alwi Zainal Abidin said the move was necessary as several aspects of the case still required further action before a final prosecutorial decision could be made.
He said the investigation paper had initially been submitted to the DPP after the suspect was arrested and remanded to assist investigations.
"However, the investigation paper has been referred back to us to complete several outstanding matters in the investigation," he said at a press conference at the Perak police contingent headquarters here today.
He added that among the pending requirements were forensic analyses that had yet to be completed.
He also said the suspect had since been released on police bail.
The case is being investigated under Section 500 of the Penal Code for defamation and Section 233 of the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998.
On June 11, a TikTok user
accused of defaming and humiliating the Tok Batin
in a livestream linked to the discovery of missing hiker Jaslinda Saludin was arrested at his home in Ipoh.
The suspect, in his 30s, had allegedly claimed that Jaslinda had been kept by the Tok Batin for two weeks before she was found by Orang Asli villagers in Kampung Lubuk Gaharu, Pos Musoh, on June 6.
Following this, 50 Tok Batin and Orang Asli representatives from three districts have lodged police reports over alleged slander on social media against Lubuk Gaharu Tok Batin
Mohd Asmmadi Abdullah
.
Jaslinda, 49, was
found safe
by villagers after going missing for 14 days while hiking Gunung Batu Putih.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Does Sedition Act apply to rebutting royals on politics, asks Pua
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Former DAP MP Tony Pua has questioned whether those who respond to members of the royal family on political matters could be subjected to the Sedition Act.
"When senior members of the royalty get openly involved directly (or even indirectly) with politics and political campaigns, demonstrating bias and partiality, are they then not subjecting themselves to rebuttals and retorts?" Pua asked in a Facebook post.
"In that case, are these members of the royalty above reproach or debate? Will the Sedition Act still apply?" asked Pua, a former three-term Petaling Jaya MP.
However, Pua did not specify which member of the royal family he was referring to.
It was previously reported that Pua was being probed over a Facebook post discussing
Malaysia's constitutional monarchy
and the limits of the powers of the Malay rulers.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Lorry driver in FRU crash freed of concealing stolen cows charge
Article:
TELUK INTAN: The lorry driver facing a charge of dangerous driving that caused the deaths of nine Federal Reserve Unit (FRU) members was acquitted and discharged by the Magistrate's Court here today of a charge of assisting in concealing two stolen cows last year.
Magistrate Naidatul Athirah Azman made the ruling after finding that the prosecution had failed to prove a prima facie case against Rudi Zulkarnain Mat Radi, 46, at the end of the prosecution's case.
Deputy public prosecutor Nur Darwis Aghniya Halim Azizi appeared for the prosecution, while lawyers Mohd Hafizuddin Khan Norkhan and Nur Intan Syakieraah Zakaria represented the accused.
On May 20, 2025, Rudi Zulkarnain pleaded not guilty to a charge of assisting in the transportation of two stolen cows, a black KK Cross bull and a white Brahman bull, belonging to Rozali Ismail, 66, from Kampung Sungai Keli, Hutan Melintang here.
The offence was allegedly committed in Kampung Kebun Baru, Hutan Melintang, at 6pm on April 14.
The charge, under Section 414 of the Penal Code, provides a maximum prison sentence of seven years or a fine or both upon conviction.
The trial, which began on Oct 15, 2025, concluded on March 17 this year, with five prosecution witnesses called to testify.
Rudi Zulkarnain had earlier claimed trial to nine counts of causing the deaths of nine FRU members in a crash on Jalan Chikus-Sungai Lampam on May 13, 2025, with the trial still ongoing.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: After Starmer's announcement on resignation, how is his successor chosen?
Article:
LONDON: Prime Minister Keir Starmer said on Monday he would resign, with a new leader to be in place by the time parliament returns in September, paving the way for Britain to have its seventh leader in 10 years.
Here's what happens next:
HOW DOES A LEADERSHIP CONTEST WORK?
Any candidate wishing to replace Starmer would need to secure the support of 20 per cent of Labour After members of parliament. With Labour currently holding 403 seats, that equates to 81 lawmakers, including the challenger.
Candidates also must hit thresholds for support from grassroots Labour Party organisations, and from affiliated organisations such as trade unions.
WHO GETS TO DECIDE THE WINNER?
If more than one candidate qualifies, the winner is decided by a ballot of all Labour Party members and affiliates. The winner then becomes prime minister.
HOW LONG WOULD IT TAKE?
Though the timeline is officially decided by the party's governing body, Starmer said nominations would open on July 9 and close before parliament goes into recess, which is scheduled for July 16.
He said if there were to be a contest, it should be completed by the time parliament returns, which is scheduled for September 1.
WHAT HAPPENS IF THERE'S ONLY ONE CANDIDATE?
If only one candidate meets the threshold for support, there is no vote: the candidate is elected unopposed as Labour leader and becomes prime minister.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Three Singaporeans escape unharmed in fatal Johor crash
Article:
KOTA TINGGI: Three Singaporean passengers were unharmed after a collision involving a tour bus and a car claimed the life of a Malaysian motorist along Jalan Johor Baru-Mersing, here today.
The fatal 7.45am crash occurred at Km57 of Jalan Johor Baru-Mersing involved the bus carrying three Singaporean men and a Honda City driven by the deceased 45-year-old man.
The bus is believed to have veered into the opposite lane before colliding with the car.
The bus, carrying passengers from Singapore, was travelling towards Tanjung Gemok Jetty, while the Honda City was travelling from Mersing towards Rapid Pengerang when the crash occurred.
District police chief Superintendent Yusof Othman said the Honda driver suffered severe head and bodily injuries and was pronounced dead at the scene.
"The bus driver and all three Singaporean passengers were not injured in the accident," he said in a statement today.
The victim's body was sent to Kota Tinggi Hospital's forensic unit for post-mortem.
Police detained the 45-year-old bus driver to facilitate investigations.
Members of the public with information on the accident were urged to contact the Traffic Investigation and Enforcement Division at the Kota Tinggi district police headquarters.
The case is being investigated under Section 41(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987 for causing death by reckless or dangerous driving.
If convicted, offenders face a jail term of between five and 10 years, a fine of between RM20,000 and RM50,000, and disqualification from holding a driving licence for not less than five years.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 5 hospitalised after mercury leak at courier facility in George Town
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: Five workers were sent to hospital after a mercury spill from a damaged parcel at a courier facility in Jalan Terengganu here today.
The incident occurred at 1.03pm when a parcel being processed at the facility was found to have leaked an estimated 500ml of mercury.
Penang Fire and Rescue Department assistant operations director John Sagun Francis said the spill was believed to have originated from a J&T Express parcel that broke during the sorting process.
"The incident involved a mercury spill estimated at 500ml from a parcel reported to have broken while undergoing sorting operations.
"Five individuals were affected and received initial treatment from Health Ministry personnel before being taken to Penang Hospital for further medical evaluation," he said in a statement.
John said firefighters from the Jalan Perak fire and rescue station, assisted by the Bayan Baru fire and rescue station's Hazardous Materials (Hazmat) team, were deployed to the scene.
He said the Hazmat team conducted an assessment before personnel wearing full personal protective equipment carried out decontamination works using sulphur powder and bleach.
Environmental pH readings were also taken to ensure the area was safe.
After confirming there were no further hazards, the operation concluded at 3.06pm.
The operation involved 17 personnel and three vehicles, comprising a fire and rescue tender, a Hazmat unit and a utility vehicle.
The Health Ministry and police also assisted in the operation.
The victims, comprised three Malaysians and two Myanmar nationals aged between 20 and 45, were reported to be in stable condition.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hate narratives against Rohingya online may lead to violence, warns criminologist
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The growing wave of hostility towards illegal immigrants online, particularly rhetoric directed at Rohingya refugees and undocumented migrants, risks normalising vigilantism and potentially violence if left unchecked.
Senior Criminologist Datuk Dr P. Sundramoorthy warned that recent social media posts calling on members of the public to "round up" undocumented foreigners, including Rohingya migrants, should be viewed with serious concern as they reflect a dangerous process whereby hostility, prejudice and exclusion become normalised within public discourse.
He told the New Straits Times that repeated exposure to messages portraying illegal immigrants or Rohingya communities as a threat to society could gradually lead some individuals to perceive discriminatory behaviour and even violence as justified responses.
Drawing on differential association theory, which suggests that criminal or deviant behaviour is learned through interactions with others and exposure to environments that normalise such actions, he said attitudes that justify unlawful conduct can develop through social influence and repeated exposure to narratives that legitimise wrongdoing.
"When narratives that justify hostility become widespread, they can influence individuals to view actions that would otherwise be considered unacceptable as reasonable or necessary," he said.
The criminologist also expressed concern over individuals posting videos online showing themselves confronting, questioning or allegedly detaining undocumented migrants while appearing to assume the role of enforcement officers.
"Immigration enforcement is the responsibility of authorised government agencies. When members of the public begin taking enforcement matters into their own hands, society risks normalising vigilantism.
What may initially be framed as community action can quickly escalate into unlawful conduct involving intimidation, harassment, wrongful confinement, assault or impersonation of public officials," he said.
Sundramoorthy said such behaviour undermines the rule of law, weakens public confidence in legitimate institutions and encourages others to bypass established legal processes.
He also described the proliferation of dehumanising language directed at Rohingya refugees and undocumented migrants as particularly alarming, citing comments suggesting Rohingya women should be treated as slaves and calls for members of the public to target Rohingya communities.
"Criminological and historical evidence demonstrates that dehumanisation often serves as a precursor to violence. By stripping individuals of their dignity and humanity, such rhetoric reduces empathy and weakens the moral restraints that ordinarily prevent people from causing harm to others," he said.
Sundramoorthy noted that similar patterns of xenophobia and moral panic have been observed globally, where migrants, refugees and minority groups were blamed for crime, economic hardship and social problems, often fuelled by misinformation, rumours and exaggerated claims amplified through social media.
While acknowledging that concerns over illegal immigration are legitimate public policy issues, he stressed that they must be addressed through lawful, evidence-based discussions rather than collective blame.
"Legitimate concerns about policy should never be used to justify hostility towards entire communities, whether Rohingya refugees or other migrant groups. Conflating individual misconduct with collective guilt risks fuelling racism, discrimination and social division," he said.
He warned that sustained online hatred could eventually translate into real-world violence, noting that research on radicalisation has shown repeated exposure to hateful narratives can create conditions conducive to both lone-actor attacks and collective violence.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ex-supermarket supervisor jailed two years for CBT involving RM568,200 TnG reload value
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A former supermarket supervisor was in tears after being sentenced to two years' jail from today by the Sessions Court after pleading guilty to 14 charges of criminal breach of trust and money laundering involving RM568,200 worth of Touch 'n Go (TnG) eWallet reload pins intended for customers.
Nur Atiqah Asyirah Muhamad Fauzi, 29, who was also a senior cashier at Aeon Max Valu Prime Desa Park City in Kepong, changed her plea nearly a year after she was first charged.
She had initially faced two counts of CBT for transferring Aeon's TnG reload pins worth RM98,800 belonging to the supermarket in 2023 and 2024 into her own eWallet.
She had also originally claimed trial to 12 counts of money laundering for transferring RM469,400 involving similar transactions to herself, her family and friends during the same period.
Today, judge Norma Ismail sentenced her to two years' jail for each charge, ordering all 14 sentences to run concurrently from today.
Dressed in a black headscarf and brown blouse, Atiqah was visibly emotional and in tears after sentencing, while her family members, including her father, who stood as her bailor, observed proceedings from the public gallery.
During proceedings, the charges were read out again in court and she was seen wiping away tears while her lawyer Arik Zakri Abdul Kadir made mitigation submissions.
Arik Zakri appealed for a two-year jail sentence, saying Atiqah, who worked as a cashier for eight years, was a dedicated worker who had previously won a best cashier award.
He said she is a first offender, remorseful and not a hardcore criminal.
"We do not take money laundering offences lightly. However, in this case, there were no elements of sophisticated layering and concealment. I believe the prosecution has handled far more complex cases than this," he said.
He argued that Atiqah was a victim of a romance scam, claiming she had been manipulated by an ex-lover into committing the offences.
"She committed the offences in order to support his lifestyle, to the extent that he was able to purchase several cars.
"Although this act is not forgivable, I submit that it should be taken into consideration. After this came to light, she was abandoned and this has served as a consequence and a lesson to her," he said.
Deputy public prosecutor Batrisyia Mohd Khusri, however, urged the court to impose a proportionate sentence, stressing the accused's position as a senior cashier entrusted with financial responsibilities, including handling an e-payment terminal.
It was not a one-off mistake but a sustained, systematic breach of trust, she said.
"She was entrusted by the company and her actions not only caused financial losses but were also carried out in an organised manner over a period of two years.
"Although she was a dedicated worker, she abused the knowledge and skills gained in the company.
"The alleged emotional manipulation and romance scam cannot be considered a mitigating factor as it does not justify the commission of the offence," she said.
She said failing to impose an appropriate sentence would send the wrong message to society that money laundering offences are taken lightly.
According to the facts of the case, on Aug 10, 2024, the supermarket manager lodged a police report against Atiqah and another staff member on suspicion of misuse of the e-payment terminal without consent.
Investigations revealed that, in her capacity as a supervisor, she had used the terminal to carry out TnG eWallet top-ups for personal use from August 2022 to July 2024.
She misused the supermarket's terminal by carrying out TnG eWallet top-ups and redeeming the value into her own account, amounting to RM98,800.
Investigations found that she also received and transferred funds not only into her own e-wallet account but also into those belonging to her mother and siblings.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Unloved and directionless, UK's Starmer quits after just two years
Article:
KEIR Starmer was once hailed as the leader who would bring pragmatism and stability to Britain after years of political chaos. When he quit as prime minister on Monday, the very lack of ideology that propelled him to power drove his downfall.
After guiding the Labour Party into power in 2024 with the biggest parliamentary majority in Britain's modern history, Starmer focused on what he believed was possible to achieve, rather than setting out a clear vision of a future Britain.
He soon came to be seen by many voters and members of his party as lacking conviction and a clear direction, more than 20 party insiders said. He had no big idea.
Without what one senior Labour lawmaker called "a guiding light", the former lawyer was buffeted by competing Labour factions, lobbied by vested interests and misunderstood by wary voters, many of whom came to hate what they saw as his indecision and his robotic performances.
TURNED TO HIS WIFE FOR COUNSEL
His policies often unravelled, resignations and sackings from his team followed, and the remaining trusted aides around him struggled to help him offer the country a clear narrative of what his government wanted to do to "change Britain."
Starmer, 63, increasingly turned to his wife Victoria for reliable advice. On May 12, five days after disastrous local election results for Labour prompted calls for him to quit, he had a long lunch with her and emerged determined to fight on.
[[nid:1469822]]
But it was a weekend away at the prime minister's country residence in Chequers with his wife that appeared to have persuaded him to change course, bend to the inevitable and resign.
On the doorstep of his Downing Street office and residence, he said he would do everything to allow an orderly transfer of power to the next Labour leader, expected to be his rival Andy Burnham, the former Greater Manchester mayor.
"The question my party is asking now is whether I am best placed to lead us into the next general election," he said in an emotional speech when his voice broke when he thanked his family foir their support.
"I have heard the answer from my parliamentary party to that question and I accept that answer with good grace."
By the end, deeply unpopular among voters for broken promises and policy U-turns, Starmer saw support drain away from him.
Even some of his most loyal allies in his top cabinet team of ministers privately urged him to allow an orderly transition of power rather than a damaging leadership contest.
His pledges to fight to save his premiership quickly evaporated after most in the party decided they could not enter a national election due in 2029 with him at the helm.
After decisively winning an election for a parliamentary seat in northwestern England, Burnham was now seen as the "Reform slayer", the politician who had a chance of keeping the populist party of veteran Brexit campaigner Nigel Farage at bay.
FEAR OF FARAGE DROVE CAMPAIGN TO OUST STARMER
"I would do anything to stop Farage," said lawmaker Catherine West, who broke cover over the May 9-10 weekend to try to force others to mount a challenge against the prime minister.
It was never meant to be this way.
[[nid:1469821]]
After becoming a Labour lawmaker in 2015 at the age of 52, Starmer was elected leader just five years later inheriting the party after its worst election showing since 1935 under his predecessor, veteran left-winger Jeremy Corbyn, beset by accusations of antisemitism and a fudged Brexit policy.
He used his experience of running the Crown Prosecution Service, an independent body which advises police and prosecutes criminal cases in court, to try to modernise the Labour Party, and ultimately make it more electable.
As when he was Director of Public Prosecutions (DPP) - essentially Britain's top prosecutor, he attacked the problem strategically - first getting rid of alleged antisemitism and tackling factionalism; putting the organisation back on its feet financially; bringing the best Labour lawmakers into his top team; and finally adopting policies to address Britain's needs.
"Everything we offer will be built on a bedrock of economic stability and a plan for growth," his spokesperson said at the time.
Initially it worked. His newly re-fashioned Labour won a large majority in Britain's 650-seat parliament, but analysts were quick to point out that the party's victory was fragile - Labour actually secured one of its lowest vote shares ever and the win was highly dependent on tactical voting.
After 14 years of infighting, Brexit battles and five prime ministers in eight years, the Conservatives had all but blown themselves up as a party.
John Curtice, Britain's best known pollster, said: "All in all this looks more like an election the Conservatives lost than one Labour won."
FRUSTRATION SET IN OVER ACHIEVEMENTS
Starting from a fragile base was not helped by the Starmer government's cautious approach to policy during campaigning and an already growing narrative that all of Britain's many problems from housing to anaemic economic growth would take time to fix.
Once in power, Starmer's government struggled first to define its policy agenda and then to implement it - focusing on growth that never really came, on reducing illegal migrant arrivals that kept on coming and on fixing a health system that kept on throwing up more challenges.
[[nid:1469820]]
One person in his top team in opposition said Labour was just not prepared for government, describing a time when they had tried to formulate policy but were told to "stop" so as not to "frighten people in advance of the general election."
"We don't have a plan for what we're going to do when we get in, if we do get in, because it might jinx it," the person remembered.
As the months went by, Starmer tried to talk up his government's achievements - improving working conditions, reducing health service waiting lists and overseeing an economic environment in which interest rates could be cut.
But despite several resets to his approach, the British leader failed to engage a wary public, with a former aide saying Starmer failed to offer "a destination" from which voters could understand or make sense of his decisions.
Instead voters could not see beyond gaffes over donations, policy U-turns and the appointment of Labour veteran Peter Mandelson despite his known connections to the late convicted US sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.
Starmer's defence that he was not told about the extent of Mandelson's ties to Epstein made many feel he was out of touch at best, and, at worst, not in control of his administration.
"It was a bad appointment," said one former aide, suggesting it had been driven through by only two other former advisers.
BLAME GAME TAINTED THE END OF STARMER ADMINISTRATION
The frustration inside his Downing Street office became more palpable.
Some aides blamed what they called a hostile right-wing media, but after one reset followed another, Starmer ultimately failed to display, as described by one adviser, "his passion for these domestic causes."
He lost some of his closest advisers, including his former chief of staff Morgan McSweeney, over the Mandelson scandal, and after sacking the top official at the foreign office, his relationship with Britain's civil service soured.
Starmer did better on the international front.
[[nid:1469819]]
On Russia's war against Ukraine, he was praised by some other European leaders for helping to spearhead the 'coalition of the willing' of nations willing to help in the event of a peace deal, and alongside French President Emmanuel Macron, spearheaded talks on reopening the Strait of Hormuz.
The British leader also had some success in winning round US President Donald Trump, often by massaging his ego - offering him a second state visit to Britain and praising his efforts to bring peace in Ukraine and an end to other conflicts.
That soon was replaced by a torrent of jibes against him from the US leader, who said he was no Winston Churchill after Starmer refused to draw Britain into the war on Iran.
On Sunday, Trump posted on Truth Social: "Keir Starmer will resign as Prime Minister of The United Kingdom. "He failed badly on two very important subjects- IMMIGRATION AND ENERGY (OPEN NORTH SEA OIL!). I wish him well!"
Perhaps his lasting legacy will be the fracturing of Britain's traditional two-party system.
The local elections in England, and parliamentary ones in Scotland and Wales showed Britain's traditional two-party system had been blown apart with Reform gaining a strong foothold across the nation.
While Labour membership numbers fell, Reform's rose, with more than 270,000 people signed up.
It was that threat, Starmer had hoped would seal support for him, telling his Labour Party in February the battle with Reform was the "fight of our lives."
It was a fight he ultimately lost.
* The writer is from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: IJM Land expands Seremban 2 commercial portfolio on sustained township demand
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: IJM Land Bhd is expanding its commercial offerings across Seremban 2, supported by sustained demand within the township and surrounding residential growth.
The property developer has begun handing over vacant possession for Phase 1 of Nexus, marking the completion of a commercial development in Seremban 2 designed to serve expanding residential catchments in Negeri Sembilan.
The handover follows the recent delivery of Ikon @ Centrio in the township core, as IJM Land continues to strengthen its commercial ecosystem within the broader Seremban 2 master plan.
Nexus enters the market at a pivotal stage in Seremban 2's growth, as the township matures with a well-established residential base and expanding surrounding communities.
"Commercial sustainability depends on real, everyday demand, and Nexus responds directly to how Seremban 2 lives, moves and grows," said Datuk Chai Kian Soon, chief operating officer of IJM Land.
"By bringing retail and services closer to expanding neighbourhoods, we are giving businesses and purchasers an asset positioned at the centre of a widening catchment, ensuring Nexus remains a valuable commercial anchor for years to come," he said in a statement.
From an investment perspective, Nexus is supported by clear market fundamentals. As a freehold commercial asset within an established and growing township, it benefits from an existing residential base, expanding surrounding neighbourhoods and direct exposure to daily demand.
Nexus is built on a 10.1-hectare (ha) freehold site with a gross development value (GDV) of RM385 million and comprises 207 units. Phase 1 spans 8.66ha with a GDV of RM300 million and consists of 166 lifestyle shop offices.
The completion comes as Seremban 2's residential precincts continue to expand beyond its established core, supported by new developments including S2 Heights and S2 Heights Aman.
"Commercial success is built on fundamentals that hold under real demand, sustained population growth and a clear role in daily life. Nexus sits exactly at this intersection," said Chai Kian Soon, chief operating officer of IJM Land.
In a statement, Chai said the development is aligned with the township's evolving structure and provides businesses with a commercial asset supported by a clearly defined catchment.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Grab's introduces 'Group Ride' to promote carpooling, sustainable transport
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Grab Malaysia is responding to the growing national narrative of
"Kongsi Perjalanan, Kongsi Tambang"
(Share the Journey, Share the Fare) by introducing'Group Ride', a new feature designed to promote sustainable transport habits among Malaysians.
The initiative aligns with the Transport Ministry's recent call for increased carpooling services to help consumers mitigate rising costs of living and a domestic fuel supply shortage.
By optimising travel routes and vehicle occupancy, the feature aims to offer a practical solution for smarter, more efficient urban mobility.
AFFORDABLE AND EFFICIENT
The Group Ride feature is built upon three core pillars: affordability, technological efficiency, and sustainability.
One of highlights of the new feature is its enhanced fare-sharing functionality, which completely eliminates the need for manual bank transfers or calculating who owes what after a trip. Passengers can choose from flexible cashless payment models depending on their preferences:
• Equal split fare:
The total ride cost (excluding tolls and surcharges) is automatically divided evenly among all passengers and charged to their respective cashless payment methods once the trip ends.
• Split fare by route:
Grab's proprietary algorithms calculate the cost proportionally, ensuring passengers traveling shorter distances pay only for the exact time and distance they consume.
• Host pays for all:
The booking host covers the entire fare for the group.
Through these optimisation and split-fare structures, passengers can significantly reduce their travel expenses by up to 40 per cent compared to standard individual bookings.
FLEXIBLE PICK-UPS AND ARRIVALS
To streamline group coordination, the feature heavily enhances user convenience by targeting two distinct travel scenarios: 'Arrive Together' and 'Depart Together'.
The Arrive Together setup allows friends starting from different pickup locations to sync their journeys seamlessly toward a single, shared destination (such as a restaurant or office), maximising convenience.
Alternatively, the Depart Together option caters to groups starting from the same pickup location who need to drop off at different individual destinations along the way. By organising these routes into a single booking, Grab manages the coordination hurdles that previously required separate app bookings.
SETTING UP A GROUP RIDE
To get started, users can set up a group ride by tapping the designated widget on the transport page. Passengers can choose between a group departure (same pickup point, different drop-off points) or a group arrival (different pickup points, same drop-off point).
The process works as follows:
Initiate the ride:
The host passenger opens the Grab app, taps the Car tile, inputs their primary starting point and destination and selects Group Rides.
Choose the pick-up or arrival :
The host selects either 'Arrive together' or 'Depart together' and sets the preferred payment option (such as an equal split or split by route).
Invite members:
The host shares a unique invite link with friends or colleagues via standard messaging platforms like WhatsApp or Messenger.
Members add stops:
Invited passengers tap the link on their updated Grab apps to join the group and input their specific pickup or drop-off points.
Finalise and book:
Once everyone has entered their stops, the host confirms the route.. Grab's system automatically sequences the stops to establish the most time-efficient route before the host selects the ride type and books.
ENHANCED TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
To ensure a seamless user experience, Grab will soon introduce a 'Waiting Room' feature, enabling group members to track vehicle progress and passenger readiness in real-time.
Despite the shift toward shared transit, Grab maintains that passenger safety remains a top priority. Group Ride is fully integrated into Grab's existing safety ecosystem, which includes the SOS button, trip monitoring technology, and a centralised safety centre.
CAMPAIGN ROLLOUT
As part of the launch, Grab is offering a 15 per cent discount on Group Rides to encourage adoption. The campaign, running under the hashtag #GrabBersama Malaysia, targets a broad segment of passengers looking to optimise their daily commutes.
Media representatives have been given the chance to experience the feature first-hand, with feedback highlighting the feature's ease-of-use and cost-savings as major plus points.
"We have been using the Group Ride feature for lunch outings, and it's helped us save on ride-sharing fares, especially when commuting to crowded lunch hotspots.
"The feature also gives users more incentive to carpool, an environmental upside that shouldn't be understated," said Jeremy Ng, an employee at Media Prima Berhad.
The Group Ride feature is now available on the Grab app, available for download
here
.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Woman assaulted at Gelang Patah food stall, police probe viral clip
Article:
JOHOR: Police have launched an investigation after a video showing a woman being assaulted by a man at a food stall in Gelang Patah went viral on social media.
The 1-minute-30-second clip, which surfaced on June 20, shows a man striking a woman at a roadside stall.
Iskandar Puteri district police chief M. Kumarasan said checks revealed that the woman is believed to be a worker at the stall operated by the male suspect.
However, the exact circumstances leading to the incident, as well as the identities of those involved, are still under investigation.
He said the case is being investigated under, among others, Section 324 of the Penal Code for voluntarily causing hurt using a weapon, Section 505(b) for statements conducive to public mischief, Section 160 of the same Act for affray, and Section 233 of the Communications and Multimedia Act for alleged improper use of network facilities to distribute offensive or harmful content.
"If convicted, penalties range from imprisonment, fines or both depending on the respective provisions," he said.
Kumarasan said police are tracking down the victim to assist in investigations and have urged the public with information to come forward.
Information can be channelled to the Iskandar Puteri district police headquarters at 07-511 3622 or its hotline at 07-511 4486.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police arrest three for supplying communication equipment to online scam syndicates
Article:
IPOH: Police have arrested three people, including two Chinese citizens, believed to be involved in supplying communication equipment to online scam syndicates.
Perak police chief Datuk Mohd Alwi Zainal Abidin said the raid was conducted at a condominium in Bandar Baru Medan here at 1.20pm on Friday.
He said the suspects, aged between 27 and 58, were arrested during the operation.
"Initial investigations revealed that the suspects were involved in supplying mobile phones and SIM cards to online scam syndicates operating in the country.
"We believe the syndicate has been carrying out these activities since early this year and charged between RM300 and RM500 for each mobile phone supplied together with a SIM card," he said at a press conference at the Perak Police Contingent headquarters here today.
Alwi said police also seized a large quantity of communication equipment and devices believed to have been used in the operation.
"Among the items confiscated were 1,335 SIM cards from various telecommunications providers, 554 microchip cards, 409 mobile phones, three laptops, five SIM box devices, two CPU units, two monitors, a router and a Jeep vehicle.
"The total value of the seized items is estimated at RM310,400," he said.
Alwi said all three suspects were initially remanded for one day under Section 117 of the Criminal Procedure Code on June 20.
He said the two Chinese nationals are currently being detained under the Immigration Act for 14 days to facilitate investigations.
The case is being investigated under Section 420 of the Penal Code for cheating and Section 120B of the Penal Code for criminal conspiracy.
Upon conviction, offenders may face imprisonment of up to 10 years, whipping and a fine.
Alwi said online scam syndicates continuously employ various methods and networks to evade law enforcement authorities.
"However, Perak police will continue to take proactive measures to eradicate all activities linked to online fraud syndicates.
"We urge members of the public not to assist these criminal networks, whether by renting out residential premises or supplying communication equipment that may be used for illegal activities," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia ramps up AI push with 60-month plan: Gobind
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia is intensifying efforts to build a comprehensive artificial intelligence (AI) ecosystem over the next 60 months, with a focus on strengthening digital infrastructure, talent, governance and industry readiness to accelerate enterprise-wide AI adoption.
Digital Minister Gobind Singh Deo said the government has rolled out a series of policies covering AI, energy and industrial development to ensure the country is prepared for the next wave of technological transformation.
"We are looking at how we can prepare our country for AI over the next 60 months. Many new policies have already been launched, including those related to energy, new industries and AI.
"We have the technology, we have connectivity, we have data centres, we have cybersecurity. We have also focused on talent.
"The question now is whether every Malaysian understands how they can use this technology. The same applies to companies," he said.
Gobind said Malaysia is targeting for the digital economy to contribute 30 per cent to gross domestic product (GDP) by 2030, up from 25.5 per cent currently, making the next five years critical to strengthening the country's AI ecosystem.
He said the government has spent the past two years laying the groundwork for AI adoption by expanding digital connectivity, developing data centres, strengthening cybersecurity and building a skilled talent pool.
His remarks come as the joint report by FIDE Forum and Accenture Malaysia today found that while 71 per cent of Malaysian banks have implemented at least one AI application, only 17 per cent of financial institutions have successfully scaled strategic AI initiatives across their organisations.
The study revealed that none of the insurers surveyed had successfully scaled strategic AI initiatives, while no participating financial institution had achieved full enterprise-wide AI integration.
According to the report, successfully scaling AI could unlock up to a 16 per cent increase in pre-tax profit for Malaysian financial institutions, representing an estimated US$1.8 billion (RM7.7 billion) in additional value.
It also estimated that AI could contribute about US$113 billion (RM480 billion) to Malaysia's economy, equivalent to nearly one-quarter of the country's gross domestic product.
Addressing concerns over AI's impact on employment, Gobind acknowledged that some entry-level roles may be affected but stressed that the government's priority is to equip workers with new skills rather than replace them.
"We have identified the sectors that are likely to be affected over the next 60 months and are now working to ensure workers are upskilled and reskilled so they can use these new technologies to improve their jobs.
"It is not just about students. We must also empower those who are already working. We want to develop simple, accessible AI learning modules that every Malaysian can use," he said.
Gobind said Malaysia's AI governance framework is intended to complement, rather than replace, existing industry regulators.
He added that the National AI Office (NAIO), which will be institutionalised on July 28, will lead the country's long-term AI strategy and governance framework.
"The National AI Office will not override existing regulators. We will work together to develop the guardrails and governance needed to ensure AI technologies are built in line with national priorities and security requirements," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: High Court increases ex-lawyer's jail term for RM392,000 CBT
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: A former lawyer, convicted of criminal breach of trust (CBT) involving RM392,000 in land sale proceeds, has had his prison sentence enhanced from two to six years by the High Court.
The High Court dismissed Subash Hassan Aboo Hanifa's appeal against his conviction on three charges under Section 409 of the Penal Code, while allowing the prosecution's cross-appeal for a heavier sentence.
Judge Rofiah Mohamad upheld the conviction on all three charges and increased the jail term to six years for each charge, to run concurrently, while maintaining the RM30,000 fine imposed for each charge.
The court had disposed of the appeals on Dec 4 last year, but the full grounds of judgment were only released recently.
According to the grounds of judgment dated June 11, the judge found that the two-year prison sentence imposed by the Sessions Court was inadequate given the seriousness of the offence and the fiduciary relationship between a lawyer and client.
Subash, a former lawyer with Hassan Subra & Associates, was convicted over three CBT charges involving RM32,000, RM100,000 and RM260,000 in proceeds from the sale of a piece of land in Pondok Upeh, Balik Pulau.
The Sessions Court on Feb 13 this year sentenced him to two years' jail and a RM30,000 fine for each charge, with the custodial terms ordered to run concurrently.
In her judgment, Rofiah rejected the appellant's claim that there had been a mistrial after the charges were amended during the trial.
She held that the defence had been given the opportunity under the Criminal Procedure Code to recall witnesses following the amendments but chose not to do so.
The judge also dismissed the defence argument that the money was being retained pending the issuance of a fresh faraid certificate following the death of the landowner, Ahmad Husain, in 2021.
She described the explanation as an afterthought, adding that Subash had more than four years to release the money to Ahmad before the latter's death.
The court added that Ahmad had repeatedly demanded payment, lodged a police report, filed a complaint with the Advocates and Solicitors Disciplinary Board, and obtained a civil judgment against Subash for RM392,000.
Rofiah said the offence had caused prolonged hardship and disappointment to the elderly landowner and his family, who never received the proceeds of the land sale.
The judge also ruled that the fact Subash is a person with disabilities and had both legs amputated did not automatically warrant a minimum or lighter prison sentence.
She said sentencing must reflect the gravity of the offence, while issues relating to facilities and care for disabled inmates fell within the responsibility of prison authorities.
Subash has filed an appeal to the Court of Appeal against the High Court's decision.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer announces resignation [WATCH]
Article:
LONDON: British Prime Minister Keir Starmer announced his resignation on Monday after fewer than two years in office in a term characterised by policy U-turns and deep public unpopularity.
"Every decision I have taken has been about putting the country I love first. That is why I will resign as leader of the Labour Party," Starmer said as he choked up in an emotional speech outside 10 Downing Street.
Starmer said the process of picking a new Labour Party leader would be launched in July and he would remain as prime minister until his successor is chosen, to be in place in September.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: APHM ready to support nationwide enlistment of private healthcare workers
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Association of Private Hospitals Malaysia (APHM) has expressed support for the Health Ministry's proposal to enlist private healthcare workers nationwide to strengthen public medical facilities.
In a statement, the association said it is ready to work with the ministry to develop a practical and sustainable framework for the initiative.
"Drawing from our experience in the private healthcare sector, APHM is keen to contribute to the conceptualisation of this effort and stands ready to collaborate with the Health Ministry in developing a practical, flexible and sustainable framework to support its implementation," it said.
To ensure the framework is viable, APHM said it will consult its member hospitals to gather feedback on operational feasibility, on-the-ground challenges and constructive recommendations.
"We look forward to working closely with the government and all relevant parties to bring this effort to fruition, and remain committed to strengthening collaboration between Malaysia's public and private healthcare sectors for the benefit of all Malaysians," the statement added.
Health Minister Datuk Seri Dr Dzulkefly Ahmad was reported as saying that the government will soon disclose more details on the possible nationwide enlistment of private healthcare workers to support public facilities.
Such an approach has already been adopted by the Selangor Health Department.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Indonesian maid in viral abuse case charged with injuring employer's toddler
Article:
JOHOR BARU: An Indonesian domestic helper linked to a viral abuse case was charged in the Sessions Court here today with abusing her employer's one-year-old child last year.
Yulia Arnida, 34, pleaded not guilty when the charge was read before judge Madihah Zainol.
The accused, who had temporary custody of the toddler, allegedly exposed the child to physical injury by pulling her hair until her head was lifted and yanking her right arm, causing bruising.
The offence was allegedly committed at a house in Jalan Ledang, Taman Johor, here, on the afternoon of Dec 21 last year.
The charge was framed under Section 31(1)(a) of the Child Act 2001, which carries a fine of up to RM20,000, a jail term of up to 20 years, or both, upon conviction.
Deputy public prosecutor Manushri Sutakhar proposed bail of RM20,000 with additional conditions, including reporting to the nearest police station once a month, surrendering her passport to the court and refraining from contacting the victim or witnesses.
Defence counsel Jason Tan Ka Yan pleaded for lower bail, saying the accused has two school-going children, aged seven and eight, in Banda Aceh, Indonesia, as well as ailing parents.
The court, however, denied bail after the lawyer informed it that his client could not fulfil the passport condition as her passport was being held by a third party.
Madihah ordered the accused to be remanded at the Simpang Renggam detention centre in Kluang until her lawyer secures temporary travel documents from the Indonesian government.
The court fixed June 29 for mention and submission of documents.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Why we need a national cross-skilling framework
Article:
LETTERS:
Malaysia has invested heavily in improving graduate employability through curriculum reforms, industry collaborations, internships, and technical training.
Yet one area that continues to receive relatively little attention is cross-skilling among university students.
As industries evolve rapidly under the influence of digitalisation, artificial intelligence, sustainability requirements, and changing business models, employers are increasingly seeking graduates who possess skills beyond their primary field of study.
A finance graduate with data analytics skills, an engineering student with business knowledge, or a tourism student with digital marketing capabilities often has a competitive advantage in the job market.
One practical and relatively low-cost solution is to encourage cross-skilling programmes during semester breaks.
Students could be given opportunities to attend short courses, micro-credentials, professional certifications, or skill-based workshops offered by other faculties within their university or could be expanded across public and private universities through a national cross-skilling framework.
For example, a finance student may enrol in a short course on coding, data visualisation, or artificial intelligence. Likewise, an engineering student may take modules in entrepreneurship, project management, or financial literacy.
Such exposure would broaden students' perspectives while equipping them with complementary skills demanded by employers.
The benefits extend beyond individual students. Cross-skilling can help bridge the long-standing gap between university education and industry needs.
Employers frequently report that graduates possess adequate academic knowledge but lack practical and multidisciplinary competencies.
By allowing students to acquire additional skills outside their core discipline, universities can produce graduates who are more adaptable, innovative, and industry-ready.
Importantly, this initiative does not require major infrastructure investments. Most universities already possess the expertise, teaching resources, and facilities needed to deliver short courses.
As Malaysia prepares its workforce for a more complex and technology-driven economy, policymakers should consider placing greater emphasis on cross-skilling as part of the nation's higher education strategy.
Sometimes the most effective solutions are not the most expensive ones.
The low-hanging fruit may already be within reach: empowering students to learn beyond the boundaries of their degree programmes.
If implemented effectively, cross-skilling could enhance graduate employability, strengthen industry relevance, and help build a more agile workforce capable of meeting the challenges of the future economy.
ASSOCIATE PROFESSOR DR RASIDAH MOHD RASHID
Dean of School of Economics, Finance and Banking
Universiti Utara Malaysia
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: AI Living and the next evolution of residential property
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Property developers have competed on a familiar formula for decades, that is location, amenities and design, with value largely determined by proximity to transport links, the quality of surrounding infrastructure, and the functionality of the built product itself.
Against this backdrop, the rise of AI Living suggests a further evolution in how property value may be defined - not just by where a development is located or how it is designed, but by the intelligence embedded within it.
Rather than functioning as static assets, homes and commercial spaces are increasingly being conceived as responsive environments, equipped with sensors, automation systems and data-driven platforms that can actively manage energy use, security, mobility and daily services in real time.
Property experts said this shift could represent a new normal in Malaysia's real estate development, as technology becomes more deeply integrated into how buildings operate and interact with occupants.
"In this framework, buildings are no longer defined solely by their structure, but by integrated systems of sensors, automation, and data-driven platforms that continuously optimise how spaces are used, managed and experienced," said  property expert Samuel Tan.
Positioned as the world's first AI and robotics-driven residences, the AI Living concept could mark a new phase in Malaysia's residential market, where competitive advantage is defined not only by location, amenities or design, but by the level of intelligence integrated into the homes themselves.
"From a property market perspective, AI Living is less a conventional residential launch than a test of whether developers can evolve into operators of technology-enabled living platforms," Tan said.
"The concept stands out because it moves beyond standard smart-home features by integrating artificial intelligence (AI), robotics and digital infrastructure into the core design of the development, rather than treating them as lifestyle add-ons," he told NST Property.
Tan said the stronger investment case lies in Malaysia's ageing demographics and the growing need for housing that can support independent living.
"The real story is not the robots. It is whether the developer can leverage its digital infrastructure to build a scalable, trusted, and commercially viable ecosystem in which AI becomes a utility for everyday life, particularly for an ageing population. That would be a far more meaningful innovation than simply adding smart devices to a home," he told NST Property.
Malaysia is ageing faster than many realise. The population aged 65 and above stood at about 8.0 per cent, or 2.89 million out of 34.2 million people, in 2025. By 2040, the Department of Statistics Malaysia estimates that more than 17 per cent of the population will be aged 60 and above.
That demographic shift is beginning to reshape demand across healthcare, financial planning and housing while strengthening the case for assistive technologies in the home.
As the country moves towards aged-nation status, features such as independent-living support, fall detection, remote monitoring and robotic assistance could become genuine housing differentiators rather than premium gimmicks.
I-Bhd non-independent non-executive director Datuk Eu Hong Chew said AI Living's elderly-care focus is aimed at addressing that structural trend, particularly as more older Malaysians seek to age in place rather than move into institutional-care settings.
The company is pushing beyond the conventional smart home pitch with AI Living, a 500-unit residential tower at i-City that it says will be built around artificial intelligence, robotics and native digital infrastructure.
Eu said the project is as much about redefining the role of a home as it is about introducing new gadgets into it.
"If you're going to leave the house, you can tell the robot to arrange for the car to wait at the lobby. Then a facial recognition system helps to inform you that your children are back from school. He said, "If a robot can do my chores, I should worry less."
"That vision places AI Living somewhere between a premium smart-home product and an early attempt to turn residential real estate into a technology-enabled service platform."
Eu said affluent early adopters may embrace robotics-assisted living, but mainstream buyers will judge the proposition on affordability, reliability and ease of use.
He added that the success of a robots-as-a-service model will depend on whether recurring subscription fees translate into tangible daily value that justifies the cost.
Trust will be another critical hurdle. Eu noted that while homebuyers are used to evaluating location, accessibility and amenities, AI Living introduces a new layer of considerations, including data ownership, cybersecurity, privacy safeguards and system governance.
The distinction between sensing and surveillance may be clear in technical terms, but convincing residents that their data is secure and that the technology will remain unobtrusive will be central to broader market acceptance.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Five held over RM77,000 scratch-and-win scam targeting elderly
Article:
IPOH: Police have arrested five people believed to be involved in a scratch-and-win scam syndicate that cheated two elderly women out of more than RM77,000 in jewellery and cash in separate incidents in Ipoh and Taiping.
Perak police chief Datuk Mohd Alwi Zainal Abidin said the suspects, aged between 22 and 40, were detained in raids conducted in Melaka and Selangor on June 19.
He said police had received two reports linked to the syndicate, involving two elderly women who were lured with promises of gold bars and lucky draw prizes.
"In the first case, reported in Ipoh on Oct 3 last year, the victim was approached by three people at a supermarket and told she had won a 150g gold bar through a lucky draw.
"She was persuaded to hand over the jewellery she was wearing, as well as her ATM card and PIN, purportedly to facilitate the prize redemption process," he said at a press conference at the Perak police headquarters here today.
He said the suspects subsequently disappeared, while RM600 was withdrawn from the victim's bank account. The victim suffered losses estimated at RM18,600.
"In the second case, reported in Taiping on June 14, the victim was contacted by a person claiming she had won a lucky draw hamper and was instructed to collect it at the Taiping Bus Station.
"Upon arriving at the location, she was invited into a vehicle occupied by three suspects and asked to participate in a scratch-and-win game displayed on a tablet device.
"The victim was informed she had won a 100g gold bar and was subsequently persuaded to surrender her jewellery to qualify for additional prizes.
"The suspects failed to return as promised," he said, adding that the victim's losses were estimated at RM59,000.
Acting on intelligence, he said police raided an apartment in Melaka at 5.30am on June 19 and arrested three men and a woman.
"A fifth suspect, a woman, was later detained at a house in Telok Panglima Garang, Selangor," he said.
Alwi said police seized nine mobile phones, nine SIM cards, a tablet device, 16 lucky draw vouchers, clothing and footwear believed to have been used in the scams, as well as two sling bags.
"The seized items are estimated to be worth RM10,000," he said, adding that all suspects have been remanded until June 23.
The case is being investigated under Section 420 of the Penal Code for cheating, which carries a maximum jail term of 10 years, whipping and a fine upon conviction.
Police advised the public not to trust lucky draw schemes that require participants to surrender jewellery, bank cards or cash in exchange for prizes.
Those with information on suspected scam activities are urged to lodge reports at the nearest police station.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Cases of foreigners marrying locals for business licences on the rise in Selangor
Article:
SHAH ALAM: Cases involving foreign nationals allegedly entering into sham marriages with local women to secure business licences are becoming a serious concern in Selangor, the state government said.
State Local Government and Tourism Committee chairman Datuk Ng Suee Lim said the practice was not new and was believed to have been taking place for more than a decade.
He said some marriages were allegedly arranged to enable foreign nationals to operate businesses and remain in the country.
"This modus operandi presents a challenge for local authorities because the business licences are registered under the names of Malaysian spouses.
"When enforcement operations are conducted, those involved often claim they are merely assisting their husbands or wives in running the business.
